concepts.title: Greetings and Survival English
concepts.summary: Small social phrases let the learner start, answer, ask for help, thank, and leave politely.
concepts.rule: Use the whole phrase clearly. A greeting or polite phrase works as one chunk, not as loose words.
commonGoofs: thank -> thank you
yes please is okay in speech, but write Yes, please.
my name Aryan -> my name is Aryan
good bye -> goodbye

pronunciationGuides.buddyHint: Stress the second part: hello.
Smile the long ee sound: please.
Keep the th soft, then say you.
pronunciationGuides.commonIssue: replacing th with t is common at first

concepts.title: Alphabet
concepts.summary: Letters are the named pieces used for reading, spelling, and handwriting.
concepts.rule: Capital and small letters can look different, but they name the same letter.
commonGoofs: mixing letter name and letter sound
b/d reversal in writing
p/q reversal in writing
writing a small letter when the task asks for a capital

pronunciationGuides.buddyHint: Say A like the start of ape.
pronunciationGuides.commonIssue: confusing A name with short-a sound

pronunciationGuides.buddyHint: Keep lips together first: B.
C is the letter name; /k/ can be the sound in cat.
pronunciationGuides.commonIssue: using letter name when a sound is requested

pronunciationGuides.buddyHint: Touch tongue behind teeth for D.

concepts.title: Vowels and Consonants
concepts.summary: Vowels shape the middle sound of many early words; consonants usually frame the word.
concepts.rule: The English vowel letters are a, e, i, o, and u. In short words, listen for the middle vowel sound.
examples: cat and cut are different because the middle vowel changes.
commonGoofs: mixing the letter name with the sound
missing the middle vowel in short words
cat -> cut when short a becomes short u
pen -> pin when short e becomes short i

pronunciationGuides.buddyHint: Open the mouth for the short-a sound in cat.
Short e is quick, like hen.
Short i is quick, like pig.
Round the mouth a little for dog.
Short u is the middle sound in pup.

concepts.title: Sentence Start and Full Stop
concepts.summary: A sentence is one complete thought. In writing, it needs a clear start and end.
concepts.rule: Start with a capital letter and end with a full stop for these early statements.
commonGoofs: i am ready. -> I am ready.
We play -> We play.
the rat bites. -> The rat bites.
using many ideas without a full stop

concepts.title: Nouns
concepts.summary: Nouns name people, animals, places, and things. In battle, nouns often name the creature or object.
concepts.rule: Use a noun when you need to name who or what the sentence is about.
commonGoofs: using an action word when the task asks for a naming word
saying run when the task asks for the creature
forgetting that place words like shop can also be nouns
fish is singular and plural in this slice

pronunciationGuides.buddyHint: Rat has the same short-a sound as cat.
Start with a clear d sound.
Owl starts with a vowel sound, so use an owl.
pronunciationGuides.commonIssue: adding a hidden y sound before owl

pronunciationGuides.buddyHint: End with one sh sound.

concepts.title: Verbs and Present Actions
concepts.summary: Verbs show actions. In the game, verbs drive spoken commands and battle moves.
concepts.rule: Use the action that fits the subject. I, you, we, and they use the base verb; he, she, it, and one noun usually add s or es.
commonGoofs: the rat bite -> the rat bites
fish run -> fish swims
he run -> he runs
they runs -> they run
bird flys -> bird flies
dog runned -> dog ran

pronunciationGuides.buddyHint: Bite has a long-i sound.
Run uses the short-u sound.
Flies ends with a z sound.
pronunciationGuides.commonIssue: saying flys as two hard parts

pronunciationGuides.soundGuide: s + k + r + short a + ch
pronunciationGuides.buddyHint: Start the cluster slowly: s-k-r.
pronunciationGuides.commonIssue: dropping the s or r cluster

concepts.title: Articles
concepts.summary: Articles sit before nouns and make the noun phrase clearer.
concepts.rule: Use a before consonant sounds, an before vowel sounds, and the for a specific known noun.
commonGoofs: a owl -> an owl
an rat -> a rat
a apple -> an apple
using the when the noun is new
forgetting that article choice follows sound, not just spelling

pronunciationGuides.buddyHint: In speech, a often sounds like a quick uh.
Use an before owl or apple.
pronunciationGuides.commonIssue: dropping n before a vowel sound

pronunciationGuides.buddyHint: Use a soft th; the marks a known noun.
pronunciationGuides.commonIssue: saying da is common at first

concepts.title: Pronouns
concepts.summary: Pronouns replace nouns so the learner can answer without repeating the full name.
concepts.rule: I, you, we, and they use the base verb; he, she, and it usually add s or es in simple present.
commonGoofs: he bite -> he bites
they bites -> they bite
she run -> she runs
it fly -> it flies
using he/she for animals when the task already says it

pronunciationGuides.buddyHint: I is always capital in writing.
Start with a light h sound.
Start with sh, not s.
They starts with soft th.

concepts.title: Plurals
concepts.summary: Plural nouns show more than one.
concepts.rule: Most nouns add s. Nouns ending in s, x, sh, or ch often add es. Words ending in consonant + y often change y to ies.
commonGoofs: boxs -> boxes
puppys -> puppies
ratses -> rats
fishs -> fish in this slice
forgetting the final s sound in speech

pronunciationGuides.buddyHint: End with a clear s sound.
After g, plural s sounds like z.
pronunciationGuides.commonIssue: saying hard s instead of z

pronunciationGuides.buddyHint: Boxes has an extra syllable.
Change y to ies in writing.

concepts.title: Adjectives
concepts.summary: Adjectives describe nouns and change the meaning of a noun phrase.
concepts.rule: Put the adjective before the noun in these early patterns. Use one clear describing word unless the task asks for more.
commonGoofs: rat big -> big rat
the rat small -> the small rat
using two adjectives when the task asks for one
forgetting the noun after the adjective

pronunciationGuides.buddyHint: Big has a short-i sound.
Keep the sm cluster together.
End with the st cluster.
Quiet starts with kw.
pronunciationGuides.commonIssue: quiet sounding like quite in the wrong context

concepts.title: Basic Prepositions
concepts.summary: Prepositions show place, position, and direction.
concepts.rule: Use the location word that matches the scene: in, on, under, behind, near, beside, or over. A preposition usually needs an object after it.
examples: The dog runs behind the rock.
commonGoofs: using on when the object is inside
using in when the creature is on top
missing the object after the preposition
behind rock -> behind the rock
beside and behind are different positions

pronunciationGuides.buddyHint: Under has two parts.
Stress the second part: behind.
Beside means next to.
Over starts with a long o sound.
